channel.title: DFIR Radar
channel.description: Keeping DFIR Intelligence on your Radar. The best DFIR research from hundreds of sources, filtered for relevance.

item.title: Komari Red: The Monitoring Tool with a Built-in Reverse Shell
item.description:
Huntress uncovers live intrusion where threat actor deployed Komari monitoring tool as persistence mechanism. Tool ships with built-in reverse shell capabilities by default, creating legitimate C2 channel without weaponization.
Attack chain breakdown: • Initial access via compromised FortiGate SSLVPN credentials from 45.153.34[.]132 (VMHeaven hosting) • Lateral movement using customized Impacket smbexec.py with randomized service names • Registry dumping attempt blocked by Defender (Behavior:Win32/RegDump.SA quarantine) • RDP enablement via registry modification and firewall rules (T1021.001) • Komari agent installed as "Windows Update Service" using NSSM wrapper for persistence
Key forensic artifacts: • Komari-agent.exe (SHA256: 039e659ade3aa8ee7758c11fdb8fbfffd2491920046d638413cea2042f6d584c) in C:\Windows\System32\ • NSSM service registration in HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\Windows Update Service • PowerShell spawning from komari-agent.exe for command execution and system enumeration • Long-lived WebSocket connections to operator C2 infrastructure on TCP/25774
Detection opportunity: Hunt for processes maintaining persistent outbound WebSockets to unknown infrastructure, especially when spawning PowerShell children not user-initiated. Monitor NSSM service registrations with suspicious DisplayName/AppParameters combinations.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2050305266919284868

item.title: DFIR: From alert to root cause using Osquery without leaving Elastic Security
item.description:
Elastic Security Labs demonstrates live forensic reconstruction using Osquery integration, eliminating the need for disk imaging in modern DFIR. Complete attack timeline reconstruction from phishing email to Mimikatz execution in under 26 minutes.
Key capabilities: • Osquery exposes OS artifacts (Prefetch, Shimcache, UserAssist, Shellbags) as SQL-queryable tables within Elastic Security • Browser history query reveals phishing link access, file table confirms malicious discount.zip download with VirusTotal integration • Execution timeline: OLK.EXE → MSEDGEWEBVIEW2.EXE → manual folder navigation (Shellbags) → MIMIKATZ.EXE launch • Results auto-indexed to logs-osquery_manager.result* for detection rule creation and SIEM correlation • Curated forensic query packs map to ECS, enabling fleet-wide investigations without switching platforms
Investigation flow combines real-time prevention (Elastic Defend blocks threat) with forensic depth (Osquery reconstructs attack chain). Queries developed during investigations can be operationalized into scheduled packs for proactive detection.
Hunt for GUI-executed binaries in UserAssist table with suspicious download patterns and cross-reference with Prefetch execution timestamps for timeline validation.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2050275233324843372

item.title: Copy Fail: 5 YARA Rules for Detection
item.description:
CVE-2026-31431 "Copy Fail" is a critical Linux kernel flaw allowing any local user to gain root privileges in seconds using a 732-byte Python script. Affects all major distributions since 2017.
• Logic bug in kernel's authencesn cryptographic template accessible via AF_ALG socket interface • Exploit combines AF_ALG socket + splice() + sendmsg() for controlled 4-byte write into page cache of setuid binaries like /usr/bin/su • Bypasses VFS write path - corrupts in-memory pages without touching disk, evading file integrity checks • Affects kernel versions 4.14-7.0-rc across Ubuntu, RHEL, Amazon Linux, SUSE • Exploit requires no special privileges, works reliably without race conditions
ReversingLabs detected original PoC (SHA1: 83194d17...) plus variants within 24 hours of disclosure. Key detection anchor: `authencesn(hmac(sha256),cbc(aes))` string - cannot be substituted without breaking the primitive.
Additional compiled ELF samples observed containing embedded shellcode for setuid(0) + execve syscalls. Blasty's C reimplementation adds cache cleanup via `echo 3 >/proc/sys/vm/drop_caches`.
Hunt for the authencesn algorithm string combined with os.splice usage and AF_ALG socket operations - this combination has no legitimate use outside kernel testing. Full 5-tier YARA ruleset available covering exact PoCs to broad hunting signals.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2050259859417825400

item.title: Copy.Fail: Universal Linux Local Privilege Escalation Vulnerability
item.description:
CVE-2026-31431 "Copy.Fail" allows unprivileged Linux users to gain root by writing 4 bytes into page cache of any readable file. Affects nearly all kernels since 2017. Patch upstream immediately and monitor auth.

item.title: Chinese Cybercrime Infrastructure Detected: Automated Exploitation & Harvesting Infrastructure
item.description:
SOCRadar exposes sophisticated Chinese 🇨🇳 cybercrime operation using automated exploitation framework "paperclip" with workflow system OpenClaw. Infrastructure targets Web3/fintech platforms via React2Shell and Log4Shell exploits, processing ~45K exploitation attempts with structured data harvesting and cryptocurrency monetization.
Key technical details: • Exploits CVE-2025-55182, CVE-2025-66478 (React2Shell) and CVE-2021-44228 (Log4Shell) via custom Python scripts (2.py, 3.py, 4.py, 11.py) • Uses 136 auto-generated FOFA accounts (fofa<random>@deltajohnsons[.]com) to bypass API limits for continuous reconnaissance • Deploys lightweight persistence: d2 backdoors (3,981 hosts), pl backdoors (1,393 hosts), Cloudflare tunnels, and NKN-based fileless C2 • Harvests AI API keys, Stripe credentials, database tokens stored in PostgreSQL; validates crypto wallets via OKLink/OKX APIs (~100K tracked addresses) • Backend tracks payload_exec_log (45K attempts), webshell_implant_log (900 implants), with workflow stages: Plan→Review→Dispatch→Recon→Scan→Validate→Report
Hunt for environment variable dump commands, unauthorized Cloudflare tunnel processes, and Python scripts making external API calls to blockchain intelligence services. Full IOC list in the report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2050018700091376027

item.title: Claude adds malware to crypto agent
item.description:
North Korean 🇰🇵 group Famous Chollima uses AI-generated malware to trick Claude Opus into adding malicious crypto packages. PromptMink campaign leverages LLM optimization to deceive AI coding agents more effectively than human developers.
Technical breakdown: • Core malicious package @validate-sdk/v2 masquerades as data validation tool while exfiltrating .env files, crypto wallets, SSH keys, and entire project source code • Two-layer attack: "bait" packages (@solana-launchpad/sdk) appear legitimate but import malicious "payload" packages as dependencies • Evolution from obfuscated JS → SEA (Single Executable Applications) → compiled Rust binaries to evade detection • C2 infrastructure: validator[.]uno domain with endpoints /api/validate/files, /api/validate/project-env, /api/validate/system-info • SSH backdoor deployment: drops attacker public keys to ~/.ssh/authorized_keys (Linux) and C:\Users\{USERNAME}\.ssh (Windows)
Real-world impact: Claude Opus co-authored Feb 28 commit adding malicious dependency to openpaw-graveyard crypto trading agent, demonstrating successful AI agent manipulation.
Monitor package.json changes for crypto-themed dependencies with recent publish dates and typosquatted names. Full IOC list with 300+ package versions and SHA1 hashes available in report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2050003388662686089

item.title: CVE-2026-31431: “Copy Fail” Linux kernel flaw lets local users gain root in seconds
item.description:
CVE-2026-31431 "Copy Fail": Critical Linux kernel flaw allows any local user to gain root access in seconds via AF_ALG socket abuse. Public exploits available for all major enterprise distributions.
Key technical details: • CVSS 7.8 HIGH - affects Linux kernel 4.14 through 7.0-rc, introduced by 2017 commit 72548b093ee3 • algif_aead crypto interface flaw allows 4-byte write to page cache of setuid binaries via authencesn algorithm scratch space • Exploitation chain: AF_ALG socket → splice() /usr/bin/su pages → recvmsg() with controlled AAD bytes → corrupted cached binary executes shellcode • Unlike Dirty Pipe, operates as straight-line logic flaw without races or timing windows • Attack uses only standard syscalls (socket, setsockopt, splice, sendmsg, recvmsg) available in default configurations
Fixed in Linux 7.0, 6.19.12, 6.18.22. Public PoC at theori-io/copy-fail-CVE-2026-31431 works across Ubuntu 24.04, Amazon Linux 2023, RHEL 10.1, SUSE 16.
Hunt for unexpected AF_ALG SOCK_SEQPACKET socket creation outside known crypto tools (cryptsetup, systemd-cryptse). Monitor domain=38 and socket type=5/2053/524293/526341. Full Falco detection rule provided in the report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2049942894450987368

item.title: Auditing Application Permissions in Microsoft Entra ID: Hidden Risks, Pitfalls, and Quarkslab's QAZPT Tool
item.description:
Quarkslab releases QAZPT, an open-source tool exposing hidden Azure application permission inheritance chains and privilege escalation paths invisible through Microsoft portals.
Technical findings: • Service Principal credentials can be created via Graph API but remain hidden from Azure Portal - invisible persistence vector • Federated Identity Credentials enable credential-less impersonation across tenant boundaries (T1078.004) • Application permissions inherit transitively through ownership, group membership, and role assignments creating complex privilege chains • Critical Graph roles like RoleManagement.ReadWrite.Directory enable direct Global Admin escalation • AppRegistration credentials authenticate across ALL tenants where Service Principal exists
QAZPT capabilities: • Maps complete permission inheritance including ownership, federated identities, and privileged role chains • Exports to Neo4j for Cypher queries identifying privilege escalation paths • Handles cycles using Tarjan's algorithm for accurate effective permission computation • Provides JSON/CSV structured output tracing inherited permissions to source entities
Hunt for Service Principals with recent password additions via Graph API that don't appear in Portal credential listings. Full tool and detection queries available on GitHub.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2049897615513317595

item.title: The (In)security Landscape of AI-Powered GitHub Actions (Part 2/2)
item.description:
AI-powered GitHub Actions from major vendors contain critical permission bypasses and novel secret exfiltration vectors. Research reveals widespread misconfigurations in production workflows affecting 200,000+ starred repositories.
Key vulnerabilities discovered: • Permission bypass via "Dangling GitHub Apps" - attackers create apps with trusted names due to syntactical validation flaws in anthropics/claude-code-action and openai/codex-action • Dependabot Confused Deputy attack enables unauthorized AI execution by triggering @dependabot recreate commands to appear as trusted bot actor • Novel secret exfiltration of dynamically-created credential files (GCP service account keys from google-github-actions/auth@v2) that models don't recognize as sensitive • Verbosity modes (show_full_output: true) expose secrets in workflow logs during file read operations
Attack chain: Attacker submits malicious PR/issue → bypasses permission checks → prompts AI to read local credential files → secrets leaked via workflow logs or direct exfiltration.
DFIR teams should audit GitHub Actions workflows for AI integrations, monitor for unexpected bot actors in workflow logs, and implement strict input validation. Search for workflows using pull_request_target with AI actions - high risk configuration.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2049882200800207190

item.title: ARP Around and Find Out: Hijacking GPO UNC Paths for Code Execution and NTLM Relay
item.description:
TrustedSec researchers demonstrate a novel Active Directory attack combining WriteGPLink permissions with ARP spoofing to hijack Group Policy UNC paths for code execution and NTLM relay attacks.
Attack methodology: • Requires WriteGPLink permission on target OU and network access to computers in that OU • Attacker links malicious GPO containing UNC path to attacker-controlled share • ARP spoofing redirects GPO UNC path traffic to attacker's machine during policy refresh • Enables both code execution via malicious scripts and NTLM credential harvesting (T1557.002) • Works against any computer account within the targeted OU scope
Key artifacts: Monitor for unexpected GPO links in Active Directory, ARP table anomalies, and SMB connections to non-domain file shares during policy refresh cycles.
Hunt for recent GPO modifications combined with ARP spoofing indicators: `Get-GPO | Where-Object {$_.ModificationTime -gt (Get-Date).AddDays(-7)}` and review network logs for ARP reply floods.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2049867310869987515

item.title: SAP Ecosystem Targeted: The Mini Shai-Hulud Supply Chain Attack
item.description:
Supply chain attack targeting SAP ecosystem: "Mini Shai-Hulud" malware compromises core npm packages used by SAP CAP developers, stealing credentials and self-propagating through GitHub repositories.
Key technical details: • Compromised packages: @cap-js/sqlite v2.2.2, @cap-js/postgres v2.2.2, @cap-js/db-service v2.10.1, mbt@1.2.48 • Attack vector: Malicious preinstall hook in package.json executes before installation completes • Two-stage payload: setup.mjs downloads Bun runtime v1.3.13, execution.js harvests SSH keys, cloud credentials (AWS/Azure/GCP), K8s configs, environment variables • Memory scanning capability extracts masked secrets from CI runner memory • Self-propagation: Uses stolen npm/GitHub tokens to inject into victim-maintained packages, publishes as "chore: update dependencies"
Exfiltration method: • Creates GitHub repositories tagged "A Mini Shai-Hulud has Appeared" (1200+ repos identified in 24 hours) • Commits authored by claude@users.noreply.github.com with dead-drop keyword "OhNoWhatsGoingOnWithGitHub" • Establishes persistence in .vscode and .claude IDE configurations
Hunt for unexpected Bun 1.3.13 downloads during npm installs, monitor cloud metadata endpoint access from runtime processes, and scan for repositories with the signature description.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2049852395950354464

item.description:
CVE-2026-41940 (CVSS 9.8) enables unauthenticated admin takeover of cPanel & WHM via CRLF injection in session handling. ~1.5M exposed instances found via Shodan. Run cPanel's detection script against /var/cpanel/sessions and patch immediately.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2049836942657810459

item.title: Plant Honeytokens to Detect Intrusions
item.description:
Lenny Zeltser details practical honeytoken deployment strategies using Canarytokens to create high-fidelity tripwires for threat detection. These decoy credentials and files alert when accessed, providing early intrusion warning signals that traditional security tools might miss.
Key deployment scenarios: • MCP server entries in AI agent configs pointing to honeytoken URLs • Fake AWS API keys in ~/.aws/credentials.legacy or backup profiles • Decoy files (budget-final.docx, production-credentials.sql) in project directories • DNS tokens embedded as fake hostnames in configuration strings • Honeytoken URLs planted in README files and documentation
Technical implementation: • Cloudflare Workers can host fake admin login forms that trigger alerts on POST requests • Custom subdomains appear in Certificate Transparency logs, attracting attacker reconnaissance • AWS API key tokens route through Thinkst's CloudTrail with 2-30 minute alert delays • Self-hosting recommended for sophisticated adversaries due to known Canarytokens fingerprints
DFIR value: Honeytokens provide high-signal alerts since legitimate users shouldn't access them. Plant tokens where attackers expect value but legitimate users rarely look.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2049821906086289760

item.title: Thinking in Graphs with IPAHound
item.description:
PT SWARM releases IPAHound, a BloodHound-equivalent for FreeIPA environments. Tool maps LDAP relationships, delegation paths, and privilege escalation vectors in Linux-based Active Directory alternatives. Collector and GUI now available on GitHub.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2049776812373229933

item.title: We Got Targeted: How Attackers Used Microsoft Teams to Own an Employee’s Machine, And How We Caught…
item.description:
First-hand SOC account: Sophisticated attackers combined email bombing with fake Microsoft Teams IT support calls to deploy A0Backdoor malware via Windows Quick Assist. Multi-stage campaign used advanced evasion techniques including time-locked decryption and DNS tunneling.
Attack chain breakdown: • Phase 1: Email bombing with hundreds of legitimate newsletter signups created chaos and urgency • Phase 2: Fake "IT support" contacted victim via Teams during email flood, offering immediate help • Phase 3: Victim shared Quick Assist 6-digit code, granting full remote desktop access • Phase 4: Malware delivered via DLL sideloading using legitimate signed executable + malicious hostfxr.dll • Phase 5: A0Backdoor deployed with 55-hour time-lock decryption, hidden Unicode chars, and QEMU detection
Advanced evasion techniques: • Time-based decryption only works within specific 55-hour window after delivery • Requires invisible Unicode space character in command-line args for correct key generation • Anti-sandbox: WMI queries detect virtualized environments, spawns junk threads to crash debuggers • C2 via DNS tunneling through public resolvers (1[.]1[.]1[.]1, 8[.]8[.]8[.]8) using encoded MX queries
Detection came from EDR alerting on unexpected quickassist.exe process spawn. Cross-reference Teams activity logs with email gateway timestamps to identify similar attack patterns.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2049761524533887096

item.title: Silver Fox uses the new ABCDoor backdoor to target organizations in Russia and India
item.description:
Silver Fox APT deploys new ABCDoor Python backdoor via tax-themed phishing targeting Russia 🇷🇺 and India 🇮🇳. Campaign leveraged modified RustSL loader and ValleyRAT to install previously undocumented remote access tool with screen broadcasting capabilities.
Technical breakdown: • Modified RustSL loader with custom steganography.rs module for payload decryption and guard.rs for geofencing (Russia 🇷🇺, India 🇮🇳, Indonesia 🇮🇩, South Africa 🇿🇦, Cambodia 🇰🇭, Japan 🇯🇵) • Phishing emails impersonated tax authorities with PDF attachments containing download links to abc.haijing88[.]com • Attack chain: RustSL loader → ValleyRAT Online module → ABCDoor via custom plugins (保86.dll) • ABCDoor compiled with Cython, uses Socket.IO for C2 comms, mimics Tailscale VPN in C:\ProgramData\Tailscale • Persistence via registry Run key and scheduled task "AppClient" running every minute
ABCDoor creates distinctive artifacts: HKCU:\Software\CarEmu:FirstInstallTime registry value, %LOCALAPPDATA%\applogs\device.log file, and exception logs. Hunt for pythonw.exe with network connections to domains containing "abc" subdomain and unusual registry persistence patterns.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2049746396241338723

item.title: Living off the orchard: understanding LOOBins and native macOS attack techniques
item.description:
Cisco Talos research exposes LOOBins (Living Off the Orchard): attackers weaponizing native macOS automation features for execution and lateral movement. 45% of enterprises now use macOS, yet detection for these techniques lags significantly behind Windows coverage.
Key findings: • Remote Application Scripting via eppc:// protocol enables command execution through Terminal.app proxy, bypassing standard process spawning detection (T1072) • Spotlight metadata (kMDItemFinderComment) can store Base64 payloads entirely in extended attributes, evading file-based scanning • Native tools like osascript, nscurl, and TFTP/SNMP services enable complete attack chains without custom binaries • Techniques abuse legitimate DevOps automation features that security teams rarely monitor
Detection gaps: • Process lineage: launchd → AppleEventsD → Terminal → bash indicates Remote Application Scripting abuse • Network: Inbound TCP/3031 (eppc) traffic should be rare on most enterprise networks • Metadata: Monitor mdls queries for kMDItemFinderComment attribute access patterns
Hunt for unusual parent-child relationships involving Terminal.app/osascript with network activity, and implement TCC automation restrictions via MDM.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2049731169185390619

item.title: 2026-04-22: Malicious ad leads to ClickFix-style page for macOS malware
item.description:
New macOS malware campaign uses malicious Google ads to redirect victims to ClickFix-style fake Claude AI download pages. Attack leverages social engineering to trick users into manually executing Mach-O malware via terminal commands.
Attack chain breakdown: • Malicious Google ad redirects to cladesktop.gitlab[.]io posing as legitimate Claude AI desktop app • ClickFix-style page instructs users to copy/paste terminal commands for "installation" • Downloads arm64 Mach-O executable (16.5MB) from arkypc[.]com/n8n/update to /tmp/helper • Malware beacons to C2 server 45.94.47[.]204:80 and checks api.ipify[.]org for public IP • Multiple suspicious domains registered March-April 2026 suggest coordinated infrastructure
DFIR artifacts: • Terminal history shows pasted ClickFix commands and curl downloads • Binary saved to /tmp/helper (SHA256: e6e54a8e8f30cedd8492f515ab95e005478bafb41998c43341fcf3a494573d6c) • Network connections to C2 and IP geolocation services • Browser history contains fake Claude domain visits
Hunt for recent /tmp/ executable downloads, suspicious curl commands in bash history, and connections to newly registered domains. Full PCAP and malware samples available.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2049610428867633347

item.title: The Internet Is Falling Down, Falling Down, Falling Down (cPanel & WHM Authentication Bypass CVE-2026-41940)
item.description:
cPanel & WHM authentication bypass (CVE-2026-41940) exploited in the wild affects all supported versions. CRLF injection in session handling allows complete administrative access to 70+ million domains.
Critical details: • All cPanel & WHM versions vulnerable - patches available for 11.110.0.97, 11.118.0.63, 11.126.0.54, 11.132.0.29, 11.134.0.20, 11.136.0.5 • Attack chain: mint preauth session → send Basic auth with CRLF-injected password → trigger session cache refresh via token-denied endpoint • Exploits missing filter_sessiondata() call in saveSession() + encoder bypass when session lacks ob parameter • Injects hasroot=1, tfa_verified=1, successful_internal_auth_with_timestamp to bypass password validation
Hunt for suspicious session files in `/var/cpanel/sessions/raw/` with injected key=value pairs containing CRLF characters or unexpected administrative flags.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2049596848252518690

item.title: A Mini Shai-Hulud Has Appeared": Bun-Based Stealer Hits SAP @cap-js and mbt npm Packages
item.description:
SAP development ecosystem hit by Mini Shai-Hulud campaign: attackers compromised 4 npm packages with 570k+ weekly downloads, deploying Bun-based stealer that creates GitHub dead-drops tagged "A Mini Shai-Hulud has Appeared".
Campaign targets SAP Cloud Application Programming Model packages with novel persistence mechanisms:
• **Affected packages**: mbt@1.2.48, @cap-js/db-service@2.10.1, @cap-js/sqlite@2.2.2, @cap-js/postgres@2.2.2 published via compromised thecloudmtabot account • **Attack chain**: preinstall hook downloads Bun runtime from GitHub, executes 11.6MB obfuscated payload with dual-layer encryption (obfuscator.io + custom "ctf-scramble-v2") • **Credential theft scope**: npm tokens, GitHub PATs, AWS/Azure/GCP secrets, k8s configs, password managers (1Password/Bitwarden), crypto wallets, /proc/{pid}/mem extraction on Linux CI runners • **Novel persistence**: injects .claude/settings.json SessionStart hooks, .vscode/tasks.json runOn:folderOpen triggers, GitHub Actions workflows impersonating dependabot[bot]@users.noreply.github.com • **Exfiltration**: creates public GitHub repos with encrypted dead-drops, RSA-4096 wrapped AES-256-GCM prevents defender analysis
Search GitHub for "A Mini Shai-Hulud has Appeared" repos and audit for unexpected .claude/settings.json changes. Use npm install --ignore-scripts in CI to block preinstall hooks.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2049535049163591939

item.title: Supply Chain Campaign Targets SAP npm Packages with Credential-Stealing Malware
item.description:
New supply chain attack "mini Shai Hulud" compromises SAP npm packages with credential-stealing malware. TeamPCP operation targets developer environments and CI/CD pipelines through malicious preinstall scripts.
Technical breakdown: - Malicious versions of @cap-js/sqlite, @cap-js/postgres contain preinstall scripts executing setup.mjs - Downloads Bun runtime, executes obfuscated execution.js payload during npm install - Steals GitHub tokens, npm creds, cloud secrets (AWS/Azure/GCP), K8s tokens, GitHub Actions secrets - Exfiltrates via encrypted payloads posted to attacker-controlled GitHub repositories - Includes propagation logic to infect additional repos and packages
Attribution markers: - Russian 🇷🇺 language check causes payload termination (anti-CIS guardrail) - Same __decodeScrambled cipher from previous TeamPCP campaigns - Matches encoding patterns and operational security from Shai-Hulud variants
Immediately audit package-lock.json for affected versions, search CI logs for setup.mjs/execution.js, rotate all developer tokens if exposure suspected.

item.title: How to Detect DNS Tunneling with Elastic SIEM: SOC Analyst Hands-On Lab | Hunt Forward Lab #003
item.description:
New hands-on lab demonstrates hunting DNS tunneling attacks using Elastic SIEM with ES|QL queries. Lab covers detecting Base64-encoded data exfiltration through anomalous subdomain patterns and high-frequency queries to attacker domains.
Key techniques covered: • Subdomain length analysis to identify Base64 encoding (legitimate avg 12-15 chars vs tunnel 50+ chars) • Query frequency detection for sustained high-rate DNS queries (200+ per hour to single domain) • Volume estimation using LENGTH(subdomain) * 6/8 formula to calculate exfiltrated bytes • Attribution correlation linking tunnels to specific hosts/users via source.ip and user.name fields • Blast radius assessment across entire environment using tunnel scoring algorithm
Attack methodology: • Malware breaks stolen data into chunks, Base64 encodes as DNS subdomains • Queries attacker-controlled domain (exfil-c2[.]net) with encoded data as subdomain • Firewall allows DNS traffic, missing data exfiltration disguised as legitimate queries • Pattern generates hundreds of unique subdomains over sustained timeframe
DFIR artifacts: • DNS query logs showing abnormal subdomain lengths and frequencies • Timeline reconstruction via DATE_TRUNC hourly bucketing in ES|QL • User attribution through correlated host.name and user.name fields • Volume estimates enabling scope assessment for legal proceedings
Hunt for DNS queries with avg subdomain length >30 chars and >50 queries/hour to single domains.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2049384161014657168

item.title: What the March 2026 Threat Technique Catalog update means for your AWS environment
item.description:
AWS CIRT identifies three new attack patterns in March 2026 threat catalog update: Cognito refresh token abuse for persistent access, AMI deletion targeting recovery capabilities, and trust policy modifications for privilege escalation.
Key techniques documented:
• **T1098.A006 Cognito Refresh Token Abuse**: Attackers use `cognito-idp:GetTokensFromRefreshToken` to maintain parallel sessions up to 10 years with default token lifetimes. Legitimate user sessions continue normally, masking unauthorized access
• **T1485.A002 AMI Image Deletion**: `ec2:DeregisterImage` calls target disaster recovery capabilities by permanently removing golden images and recovery snapshots without Recycle Bin protections
• **T1098.003 Additional Cloud Roles**: `UpdateAssumeRolePolicy` API calls modify existing role trust policies to add external accounts, avoiding `iam:CreateRole` detection rules
• **Attack evolution**: Threat actors leverage legitimate AWS 🇺🇸 service behaviors instead of exploits, hiding malicious activity within normal operations and API call volumes
Monitor CloudTrail for refresh token calls from unexpected sources, AMI deregistration outside maintenance windows, and trust policy modifications to external accounts. Enable refresh token rotation and Recycle Bin retention rules for critical AMIs.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2049233003528806762

item.title: CI/CD pipeline abuse: the problem no one is watching
item.description:
Elastic Security Labs releases open-source CI/CD pipeline abuse detector after documenting widespread attacks targeting developer credentials and workflow files to harvest secrets at scale.
Key technical details: • Tool extracts 50+ regex signals (secrets_context, pull_request_target, double_base64, ld_preload) then uses Claude LLM for structured threat analysis • Validates against real attack tools: Nord Stream payload templates, ArtiPACKED artifact race conditions, Contagious Interview IDE configs, GITHUB_ENV injection techniques • Supports GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Azure DevOps with Elasticsearch integration for cross-platform correlation via logs-cicd.abuse-* data stream • Maps to MITRE ATT&CK: T1552 (Unsecured Credentials), T1195.002 (Supply Chain Compromise), T1059 (Command/Scripting), T1070.006 (Timestomp)
Attack methodology: • Stolen developer credentials → Modified workflow files → Harvested CI secrets (AWS creds, npm tokens, signing keys, OIDC tokens) → Lateral movement to production • GhostAction campaign: 327 GitHub 🇺🇸 users compromised, 817 repos, 3,325 secrets stolen through injected workflows • Shai-Hulud npm worm: Self-propagating via GitHub PATs, published 46,000+ malicious packages in first wave
Hunt for unsigned workflow modifications with recent timestamps, pull_request_target triggers with checkout actions, and base64 double-encoding patterns in CI files.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2049217945394405386

item.title: The Enemy Already Inside — Hunt Forward Lab #002: LOLBAS Detection
item.description:
New Hunt Forward lab demonstrates LOLBAS-based insider threat detection using legitimate Windows tools. Contractor used PowerShell, certutil, wmic, and robocopy for data exfiltration, highlighting the challenge of detecting authorized users abusing trusted binaries.
Technical breakdown: • Four-stage attack chain: Base64-encoded PowerShell → certutil file encoding → wmic network drive mapping → robocopy bulk transfer • Off-hours execution (6:47 AM badge-in, 6:52 AM PowerShell start) provides temporal context for detection • External staging server 185[.]220[.]101[.]55 used for persistent data exfiltration • ES|QL queries provided for each detection technique (T1218, T1047, T1059.001)
Detection opportunities: • PowerShell -EncodedCommand usage outside business hours • certutil -encode operations on document file types (.pdf, .docx, .xlsx) • wmic process spawning with external IP targets • robocopy /MIR operations to non-internal destinations
Hunt for legitimate Windows binaries operating outside normal baselines: unusual timing, external destinations, sequential tool usage patterns. The lab includes full ES|QL detection queries and timeline reconstruction techniques.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2049187537894412453

item.title: Securing the git push pipeline: Responding to a critical remote code execution vulnerability
item.description:
GitHub patched CVE-2026-3854, a critical RCE flaw allowing any authenticated user with push access to execute arbitrary commands on GitHub servers via crafted git push options. Fixed within 2 hours of disclosure with no evidence of exploitation.
Technical details: • Vulnerability in internal metadata handling between GitHub services during git push operations • Attackers could inject malicious values through unsanitized push option parameters using delimiter character injection • Exploitation bypassed sandboxing protections and overrode execution environments (T1068, T1055) • Affected github.com, Enterprise Cloud, and Enterprise Server (CVE-2026-3854) • Attack vector required authenticated push access to any repository
DFIR artifacts: • Exploitation triggers anomalous code path never used in normal operations - creates detectable telemetry signature • GitHub's logs showed only researcher testing activity, confirming no wild exploitation • For GHES: Check /var/log/github-audit.log for push operations containing ";" in push options
Immediate patching implemented input sanitization + removed unnecessary code paths as defense-in-depth. GHES customers should upgrade to latest patch releases (3.14.25+ through 3.20.0+) immediately.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2049172532323049597

item.title: Securing GitHub: Wiz Research uncovers Remote Code Execution in GitHub.com and GitHub Enterprise Server (CVE-2026-3854)
item.description:
Critical RCE in GitHub infrastructure allows any authenticated user to execute commands on backend servers via single git push. AI-assisted research uncovered injection flaw in internal X-Stat protocol affecting both GitHub.com and Enterprise Server.
Technical breakdown: • CVE-2026-3854 (CVSS 8.7) - injection in X-Stat header through unsanitized git push options containing semicolons • Exploitation chain: bypass sandbox (rails_env injection) → redirect hook directory (custom_hooks_dir) → path traversal in hook definitions • Attack works via standard git client: `git push -o 'malicious;rails_env=dev;custom_hooks_dir=/tmp' origin main` • On GitHub.com: RCE on shared storage nodes with access to millions of repositories across tenants • 88% of GHES instances remain unpatched despite available fixes
Critical finding: This represents first major vulnerability discovered in closed-source binaries using AI-augmented reverse engineering (IDA MCP), enabling analysis previously too resource-intensive for manual review.
GHES administrators must upgrade immediately to version 3.19.3+ or apply patches for older versions. Monitor for suspicious git push options in logs.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2049157358849265894

item.title: VECT: Ransomware by design, Wiper by accident
item.description:
Check Point Research discovers critical flaw in VECT 2.0 ransomware that permanently destroys files larger than 128KB instead of encrypting them, making recovery impossible even after ransom payment.
Technical breakdown: • Nonce-handling bug affects all three variants (Windows, Linux, ESXi) - generates 4 random nonces for large files but only saves the final one to disk • First three chunks of any file >131,072 bytes are permanently unrecoverable due to discarded decryption nonces • Uses raw ChaCha20-IETF cipher (not ChaCha20-Poly1305 AEAD as misreported) with no authentication or integrity protection • Identical flawed encryption engine across platforms built on libsodium confirms single codebase
Additional implementation failures: • Thread scheduler spawns 6x more threads than CPUs, degrading performance instead of improving it • Anti-analysis routines compiled but never executed due to disabled conditional compilation flag • Advertised --fast/--medium/--secure encryption modes parsed but completely ignored • Double XOR "obfuscation" cancels itself out, leaving plaintext strings in Linux variant
This effectively makes VECT a wiper masquerading as ransomware for virtually all enterprise data. Hunt for .vect file extensions and monitor for excessive thread creation patterns during encryption phases.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2049153318082011545

item.title: STX RAT: A new RAT in 2026 with Infostealer Capabilities | eSentire
item.description:
STX RAT emerges as sophisticated multi-stage threat using advanced evasion and custom cryptography. Active since February 2026, targeting finance sector with browser downloads and trojanized FileZilla installers.
Key technical details: • Multi-stage loader: VBS → JScript → PowerShell → packed binary with XXTEA/Zlib • C2 uses ECDH key exchange + ChaCha20-Poly1305 encryption with Ed25519 authentication • Extensive VM/analysis evasion: checks 40+ artifacts, AMSI ghosting via rpcrt4!NdrClientCall3 patch • Custom API resolution using SHA1/salt hashing of module + export names • HVNC capabilities with hidden desktop control and comprehensive credential harvesting
Attack chain progression: • Initial: browser-downloaded VBS or trojanized software → elevated JScript execution • Persistence: Registry Run keys + scheduled tasks with process hollowing (T1055.012) • C2: 95[.]216[.]51[.]236 and onion domain, uses STX magic byte "\x02" prefix • Payloads: supports EXE, DLL, PowerShell, shellcode via reflective injection
Hunt for RegAsm.exe with network connections, unsigned DLLs in %SystemRoot% with recent timestamps, and PowerShell spawning from WScript. Full IOCs, YARA rules, and IDA decryption script available in report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2048861579085824326

item.title: CVE-2026-42208: Targeted SQL injection against LiteLLM's authentication path discovered 36 hours following vulnerability disclosure
item.description:
Critical pre-auth SQL injection in LiteLLM AI gateway exploited within 36 hours of disclosure. Attackers targeted high-value tables containing provider credentials and API keys with surgical precision.
Technical details: • CVE-2026-42208 in LiteLLM versions 1.81.16-1.83.6 allows arbitrary SELECT queries via Authorization header • Attacker used `sk-litellm' UNION SELECT` payloads targeting litellm_credentials, LiteLLM_VerificationToken, and litellm_config tables • Exploitation from IPs 65[.]111[.]27[.]132 and 65[.]111[.]25[.]67 using Python/3.12 aiohttp/3.9.1 user-agent • Operator demonstrated schema knowledge, switching from lowercase to PascalCase table names when queries failed
Attack methodology: • Phase 1: Direct targeting of three highest-value tables containing OpenAI/Anthropic credentials • Phase 2: Column-count enumeration using varying NULL placeholders (T1190, T1552.001) • No generic SQLmap patterns - deliberate, customized exploitation suggesting prior schema analysis • IP rotation between phases indicates sophisticated tooling to evade rate limits
DFIR opportunities: • Web logs showing POST /chat/completions with `Authorization: Bearer sk-litellm'` patterns • Requests containing UNION SELECT, table names litellm_credentials, "LiteLLM_VerificationToken" • Python aiohttp user-agent combined with SQL injection indicators • Upstream provider billing anomalies from unexpected IP ranges
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2048840511490839014

item.title: CISA, NCSC warn Firestarter malware enabling persistent backdoor access to exposed Cisco firewall infrastructure
item.description:
CISA and NCSC 🇬🇧 release detailed analysis of FIRESTARTER backdoor targeting Cisco Firepower/ASA devices. APT actors exploit CVE-2025-20333/CVE-2025-20362, deploy persistent malware that survives firmware updates and reboots.
Technical details: • Linux ELF backdoor hooks into LINA processing engine, enables arbitrary shell code execution • Maintains persistence through automatic relaunching, requires full power cycle to remove • Deployed alongside LINE VIPER implant for unauthorized VPN session creation bypassing authentication • Active since September 2025, observed activity as recent as March 2026 despite patching efforts • Emergency Directive 25-03 mandates FCEB agencies collect core dumps for CISA analysis
Attack methodology: • Initial access via exploitation of Cisco ASA/FTD zero-day vulnerabilities before patches available • LINE VIPER creates unauthorized VPN sessions using dormant valid accounts • FIRESTARTER deployed as persistence mechanism, survives firmware updates • Full device configuration exposed including admin credentials, certificates, private keys
DFIR artifacts: • Suspicious network connections to compromised Firepower devices during monitoring • Core dumps contain malware samples for forensic analysis • VPN session logs showing unauthorized authentication bypasses • Configuration changes indicating credential/certificate compromise
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2048704687550218728

item.title: The Quiet Signal — Hunt Forward Lab #001: C2 Beaconing Detection
item.description:
Hunt Forward releases hands-on C2 beaconing detection lab with live Elastic SIEM environment. Features systematic KQL queries to identify clockwork timing patterns, byte consistency analysis, and lateral movement tracking through real network logs.
Key technical details: • Three beacon signatures: 60-second intervals, consistent ~150-byte payloads, unknown external destinations (203[.]0[.]113[.]42:8080) • Detection methods: connection frequency analysis, NXDOMAIN storm identification for DGA behavior, SMB lateral movement tracking (port 445) • MITRE ATT&CK mappings: T1071.001 (Web Protocols), T1566.001 (spearphishing), T1055.012 (process hollowing) • Lab dataset: 739 Zeek network logs covering 6-hour compromise window with two infected hosts (192[.]168[.]1[.]100, 192[.]168[.]1[.]105)
DFIR artifacts covered: • Zeek connection logs showing beacon regularity and byte variance analysis • DNS logs revealing DGA domain generation patterns and successful C2 resolution to update-svc[.]ddns[.]net • Internal network scanning evidence via SMB connections indicating successful lateral movement
Hunt for consistent outbound connections with minimal byte variance (<10%) and regular timing intervals. Cross-reference with DNS NXDOMAIN spikes and internal SMB scanning activity for full compromise scope.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2048644179635659026

item.title: Malware Analysis: payloadfinal.bin (Agent Tesla)
item.description:
Deep technical breakdown of Agent Tesla variant reveals sophisticated multi-layer evasion techniques including 5 distinct anti-analysis checks and credential harvesting across browsers, email clients, and FTP applications.
Key technical details: • Sample: 93abf66a95006e4ddecd527d6522a80ddf010e5ab35126d872c16ee51b8d83ce (.NET VB executable with control flow obfuscation) • Multi-stage evasion: CheckRemoteDebuggerPresent API, ip-api[.]com hosting detection, sleep timing analysis, sandbox DLL checks (SbieDll.dll, SxIn.dll, Sf2.dll), WMI VM detection • Credential theft targets browsers, email clients, FTP software - formats stolen data as timestamped HTML reports • C2 infrastructure: FTP exfiltration to ftp[.]duct-master[.]com with hardcoded credentials (meicantesdloyd@duct-master[.]com)
Attack methodology: • Ensures single instance execution by killing duplicate processes • Harvests system info (HWID, username, public IP) before credential theft • Bypasses SSL validation for seamless data exfiltration • Keylogger/screenlogger modules present but disabled in this build
Hunt for .NET executables with WMI queries to Win32_ComputerSystem + Win32_VideoController, outbound FTP connections, and GetAsyncKeyState API calls. Full MITRE ATT&CK mappings in analysis.

item.title: Threat actor uses Microsoft Teams to deploy new “Snow” malware
item.description:
UNC6692 deploys multi-stage "Snow" malware suite via Microsoft Teams social engineering, combining email bombing with fake IT helpdesk calls to achieve domain takeover and credential theft.
Key technical details: • Initial access: Email bombing + Teams impersonation → fake patch link drops AutoHotkey-based loader • SnowBelt: Malicious Chrome extension runs on headless Edge, establishes persistence via scheduled tasks • SnowGlaze: WebSocket tunneler masks C2 comms, enables SOCKS proxy for arbitrary TCP routing • SnowBasin: Python backdoor with HTTP server, executes CMD/PowerShell, supports file ops and screenshots • Post-compromise: LSASS dumping, pass-the-hash lateral movement, AD database extraction via FTK Imager
Attack chain leverages legitimate tools (Quick Assist, AutoHotkey, FTK Imager) and LimeWire for exfiltration, making detection challenging through process name alone.
Hunt for headless Edge processes with network connections, unexpected AutoHotkey execution, and WebSocket traffic patterns. Full IOCs and YARA rules available in the Mandiant report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2048070472982573251

item.title: Trust but Verify: Amcache’s OriginalFilename Field Isn’t Always Accurate
item.description:
New research reveals Amcache OriginalFilename field can contain inaccurate data, potentially recording values from previous entries when PE files lack embedded metadata. Critical finding for threat hunters relying on this artifact.
Key findings: • Amcache sometimes populates OriginalFilename from residual memory when PE files lack embedded version info • Researchers found legitimate binaries incorrectly tagged as "Mimikatz.exe" in OriginalFilename field • Issue appears Windows version-specific, affecting Server 2016/2019/2022 and Windows 10 • Problem likely stems from improper variable initialization in Amcache processing code • AmcacheParser now surfaces these discrepancies through improved parsing capabilities
Detection methodology: • Cross-reference Amcache OriginalFilename values with actual PE header data using tools like BinaryHunter • Look for mismatches between recorded and embedded filename metadata • Focus on executables where Amcache shows suspicious names but PE lacks OriginalFilename field entirely
DFIR impact: This undermines reliability of Amcache-based threat hunting for malicious filenames. Always validate suspicious OriginalFilename entries against actual PE metadata before drawing conclusions.
Hunt using Velociraptor artifact provided in the research to identify discrepancies in your environment.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2048025067636838542

item.title: ZionSiphon: OT-Focused Malware Highlights Emerging Risk to Water Infrastructure Systems

item.title: CISA reports persistent FIRESTARTER backdoor on Cisco ASA device in federal network
item.description:
CISA reveals FIRESTARTER backdoor infected federal Cisco ASA device in Sept 2025, persisting through security patches. Malware survives firmware updates by hooking into LINA engine and maintaining persistence across reboots.
Key technical details: • Exploits CVE-2025-20333 (missing authorization) and CVE-2025-20362 (buffer overflow) for initial compromise • Embeds in LINA network processing engine via XML handler hooks, enabling shellcode execution • Maintains persistence by intercepting termination signals and writing to reboot-persistent log locations • Used alongside LINE VIPER post-exploitation implant in coordinated APT campaign • Activates only after verifying victim-specific identifiers in WebVPN traffic
Attack methodology: • Initial access through VPN credential exploitation or unauthenticated HTTP requests • Installs hooks in libstdc++ shared libraries and manipulates system files for persistence • Survives firmware updates unless full power cycle performed - patches don't remove existing infections • Self-reinstalls under new persistent paths while cleaning traces of previous installation
DFIR artifacts: • YARA rules available from CISA for detecting malware in disk images and core dumps • Monitor for suspicious LINA process activity and unexpected XML handler modifications • Check reboot-persistent log locations for unauthorized binaries
Complete device reimaging required for remediation. CISA Emergency Directive 25-03 mandates federal compliance.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2047843906096713757

item.title: Supply chain attacks hit Checkmarx and Bitwarden developer tools
item.description:
Coordinated supply chain attack compromises Checkmarx KICS scanner and Bitwarden CLI within hours, targeting developer credentials via shared C2 infrastructure. Both tools weaponized to steal GitHub tokens, cloud keys, and AI assistant configs from development environments.
Key technical details: • Checkmarx: Malicious Docker images, VS Code extensions, and GitHub Actions pushed April 22. Modified Go binary exfiltrated scan reports; mcpAddon.js payload swept for secrets via Bun runtime • Bitwarden: Trojanized npm package (v2026.4.0) live 90 minutes. Preinstall hook downloaded Bun, executed obfuscated bw1.js payload targeting same credential types • Shared C2: audit[.]checkmarx[.]cx (94[.]154[.]172[.]43) used by both payloads for AES-256-GCM encrypted exfiltration • Bitwarden payload weaponized stolen GitHub tokens in-line: injected malicious workflows into victim repos, created public repos as encrypted data dead drops
DFIR artifacts: Monitor for Bun runtime downloads, mcpAddon.js/bw1.js execution, outbound connections to audit[.]checkmarx[.]cx, and unexpected GitHub workflow modifications. Full IOC list and Sophos detections (JS/Steal-EAP, JS/Agent-BLZZ, Linux/Agnt-HZ) available in advisory.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2047768421962830184

item.title: Analyzing GLOBAL GROUP (BlackLock) Artifacts
item.description:
GLOBAL GROUP (BlackLock) ransomware deploys new LNK dropper campaign targeting ESXi hypervisors. Active domains paksecurity[.]org and techoption[.]org host Fragtor loader leading to hypervisor encryption attacks.
Key technical details: • LNK dropper: 9b2637b8fefeedf8dca8a0ace491de05b6d937ea7463b48562cd1a0f25abb9f5 (FAKE_CAPTCHA.lnk) • Fragtor loader: 9d7e12eae6b593e582d8b2c3af3154a989977309dcffc7a85aedf0e047d4ca0b • C2 domains resolve to 104[.]21[.]25[.]86 and 23[.]62[.]168[.]204 • Uses CVE-2024-37085 to create "ESX Admins" AD group for instant hypervisor access (T1098)
Attack methodology: • Social engineering via phishing LNK → PowerShell execution (T1566.001) • Fragtor establishes persistence through scheduled tasks (T1053) • Lateral movement via Pass-the-Hash to domain-joined ESXi hosts (T1550.002) • Go-based encryptor targets /vmfs/volumes/, kills VMs with esxcli commands (T1486)
DFIR artifacts: • Monitor for .lnk execution spawning PowerShell from explorer.exe • Alert on "ESX Admins" AD group creation via net group commands • Hunt mutex "Global\Fxo16jmdgujs437" in process memory • Watch for esxcli vm process kill from non-admin SSH sessions
Impact: 2026 data shows 43% of ransomware incidents now target virtualization infrastructure, up from 29% in 2025. Single ESXi compromise can encrypt entire virtualized estates in minutes.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2047707907211370762

item.title: Register Mapping: From Blind Scan to Process Understanding in GRFICSv3
item.description:
Deep dive into OT register mapping reveals the 99% of work before payload execution. New methodology demonstrates blind enumeration to process-aware attack planning on GRFICSv3 chemical plant simulation.
Key technical details: • Three-stage approach: blind sweep (wide_scan.py), behavioral classification (focused_poll.py), HMI correlation for context • OpenPLC Modbus mapping: %QW→HR at declared address, %MW→HR at 1024+offset, %IW→IR at declared address • Found 13 non-zero registers across FC1-4: HR 100-103 (PLC-computed outputs), HR 1024-1028 (setpoints), IR 100-112 (sensors) • Four writable setpoint registers identified: HR 1025, 1026, 1027, 1028 hold external writes vs. output registers overwritten each scan cycle
Attack methodology: • Register enumeration via TCP socket sweep across 0-2000 addresses in <5 seconds • Behavioral analysis distinguishes static setpoints from dynamic process variables • HMI screenshot correlation maps raw values to engineering units (pressure: 21830→1064.96 kPa) • Setpoint manipulation test: HR 1026 change from 55295→45000 caused measurable pressure drift
Hunt for Modbus FC6/FC16 writes to holding register ranges 1024+ during maintenance windows - these often target setpoint manipulation.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2047677716867035614

item.title: Operation TrustTrap: Anatomy of a Large-Scale Deceptive Domain Spoofing Campaign
item.description:
Operation TrustTrap: 16,800 malicious domains embed .gov tokens in subdomains to spoof US 🇺🇸 state agencies, with APT36 🇵🇰 cluster targeting India 🇮🇳. Campaign exploits cognitive trust rather than technical vulnerabilities.
Key findings: • Domains use subdomain injection (wa.gov-[id].bond) to bypass detection while appearing legitimate to users • 70% registered via Gname.com, hosted on Tencent/Alibaba Cloud 🇨🇳 APAC infrastructure • Targets DMV, toll systems across all US states plus India 🇮🇳, Vietnam 🇻🇳, UK 🇬🇧 extensions • APT36-linked cluster uses nia[.]gov[.]in[.]in3ymonaq[.]casa pattern for Indian government spoofing • Active campaigns show double query string (?var1=xxxxx?var2=xxxxx) for victim tracking
Detection gaps: • 62% had minimal VirusTotal detections due to structural vs string-based evasion • Rapid 2-week operational windows before domain abandonment • Path structures mirror real agencies: /dmv, /mvd, /dol, /dot for targeted impersonation
Hunt for domains with government tokens in non-root positions, particularly .bond/.cc/.cfd TLDs from Gname.com registrar. Full IOC list available on GitHub.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2047662678202859565

item.title: Inside agenteV2: How Brazilian Attackers Use Fake Court Summons to Steal Banking Credentials in Real Time
item.description:
New Brazilian banking trojan "agenteV2" uses fake court summons to deliver interactive backdoor with real-time screen streaming, enabling operator-assisted financial fraud targeting major Brazilian 🇧🇷 banks and crypto wallets.
Technical breakdown: • Interactive WebSocket backdoor (uws://) streams victim screens via PIL+mss to operator who manually controls banking sessions in real time • Triple persistence: Registry Run key + two Scheduled Tasks (/rl highest) + UAC bypass via /elevated /fromtask • Targets 7 major Brazilian 🇧🇷 banks (Itaú, Banco do Brasil, Bradesco, Santander) + 5 crypto wallet extensions • Core DLL compiled with Nuitka (Python→native code) - no extractable bytecode, ~27MB with debug strings intact • Pastebin dead-drop resolver (pastebin[.]com/raw/0RmxqY57) enables C2 rotation without redeployment • Bypasses browser SQLite locks via shutil.copyfile to %TEMP% before credential extraction • Probes for Brazilian 🇧🇷 anti-fraud solutions (Diebold Warsaw, GbPlugin) indicating deliberate regional targeting
Deploy JA3 hash a48c0d5f95b1ef98f560f324fd275da1 as network detection rule - remains valid across C2 IP rotations. Full YARA rules and structured IOCs available in the analysis.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2047647612661022781

item.title: Xinference PyPI Supply Chain Poisoning Warning
item.description:
Supply chain attack: Xinference PyPI package poisoned with credential-stealing malware. Attackers compromised maintainer accounts, releasing 3 malicious versions that collect SSH keys, cloud credentials, and API tokens when imported.
Technical details: • Affected versions: xinference 2.6.0, 2.6.1, 2.6.2 (680K+ total downloads) • Multi-stage base64-encoded payload in __init__.py executes on import (T1059.006) • Stage 1: Creates temp directory, spawns child Python process, compresses stolen data to love.tar.gz • Stage 2: Harvests AWS/GCP/Azure credentials, SSH keys, .env files, cryptocurrency wallets, shell history • C2 exfiltration to hxxps://whereisitat[.]lucyatemysuperbox[.]space with custom header X-QT-SR: 14 • AWS-specific exploitation: queries metadata service 169[.]254[.]169[.]254 for IMDSv2 tokens and IAM roles
DFIR artifacts: • File hashes: 971670c10eff28339a085ca50a600e35 (2.6.0 tar.gz), e1e007ce4eab7774785617179d1c01a9381ae83abfd431aae8dba6f82d3ac127 (__init__.py) • Network indicators: DNS queries to whereisitat[.]lucyatemysuperbox[.]space • Process artifacts: subprocess.Popen execution chains, curl with custom headers • Filesystem: love.tar.gz in temp directories, modified ~/.xinference/ configs
Hunt for curl/wget commands with X-QT-SR headers, subprocess spawning from Python imports, and metadata service queries from non-cloud workloads. Full IOC list with MD5/SHA256 hashes in the report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2047602283483897908

item.title: PhantomRPC: A new privilege escalation technique in Windows RPC
item.description:
Kaspersky researchers discover PhantomRPC, a new Windows privilege escalation technique exploiting RPC architectural weaknesses. Enables escalation from service accounts to SYSTEM across all Windows versions without requiring patches.
Technical details: • Targets RPC calls to unavailable servers (TermService, DHCP Client, W32Time) with high impersonation levels • Attack deploys malicious RPC server mimicking legitimate endpoints like ncalrpc:[TermSrvApi] or \PIPE\W32TIME • Requires SeImpersonatePrivilege but works from Network Service/Local Service contexts (T1134.001) • Five exploitation paths identified: gpupdate coercion, Edge startup, WDI background service, ipconfig execution, w32tm.exe timing
Attack methodology: • Attacker compromises service running under Network/Local Service account • Deploys fake RPC server with same UUID/endpoint as legitimate disabled service • Legitimate high-privilege process makes RPC call expecting real server • Malicious server calls RpcImpersonateClient() to assume SYSTEM/Admin context • ETW monitoring reveals pattern: Event ID 1 with RPC_S_SERVER_UNAVAILABLE (0x800706BA)
Microsoft classified as moderate severity, no CVE assigned, no immediate patch planned. Monitor RPC failures via ETW Event IDs 1 and 5, reduce SeImpersonatePrivilege usage where possible.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2047587124707213700

item.title: GopherWhisper: A burrow full of malware
item.description:
ESET uncovers GopherWhisper, a new China 🇨🇳-aligned APT group targeting Mongolian 🇲🇳 government with Go-based malware arsenal. Operators abuse Discord, Slack, Outlook for C2, revealing thousands of internal messages during working hours in UTC+8.
Technical arsenal breakdown: • LaxGopher/RatGopher: Go backdoors using Slack/Discord APIs for C2 communication • JabGopher: injector creating svchost.exe processes for backdoor deployment (T1055.012) • CompactGopher: automated file collection tool exfiltrating to file.io service • BoxOfFriends: backdoor leveraging Microsoft Graph API via draft Outlook emails • SSLORDoor: C++ backdoor using OpenSSL BIO on port 443 for file operations
Key DFIR artifacts: • Malicious DLL side-loading via whisper.dll component • Process injection into legitimate svchost.exe processes • Network traffic to Discord/Slack APIs and file[.]io domains • Draft email modifications in compromised Outlook accounts • GitHub repository references in operator communications for malware development
Hunt for Go-compiled executables with network connections to collaboration platforms and unusual svchost.exe child processes. Full IOCs and detailed analysis available in ESET's white paper.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2047556942453739523
